content: Tired of doom scrolling through social media and seeing either other people who are young, don’t seem to be working but are far richer and more fitter than you will ever be? Or someone telling some bullshit story about what is wrong about your country and we need to “take back” our country? Yeah, me too. Here is my contribution into the void: two minute stories that is not grounded in any reality or related to “current events”. Why two minutes? Well, thanks to Tik-Tok, everyone attention span is equivalent of a toddler. You won’t get anything from this site except 2 minute bullshit stories that most likely will waste your time. But, we always like to throw away our time right? All stories are written by AI but curated by me. We serve the best bullshit AI can write. Site Admin
title: About This Site

content: Dr. Marcus Vance stared at the waveform on his monitor. For six months, the Borehole-4 acoustic logs had yielded nothing more than the low, monotonous groan of shifting tectonic plates two miles beneath the Siberian permafrost. It was tedious work, funded by a geological grant that was rapidly running out of patience. It was 3:00 AM. Running on three hours of sleep and far too much espresso, Marcus applied a new experimental neural-net audio filter—a reckless tweak in noise cancellation meant to strip away ambient rock resonance. The jagged, chaotic green lines on the screen didn’t just clear up; they organized. The tectonic groan melted away, replaced by something rhythmic, structured, and unmistakably artificial. A sequence of precise electronic tones, followed by a sudden frequency shift. Marcus held his breath, leaning closer to his monitor. He turned up the volume on his studio speakers and hit play. Out of the speakers came a voice. It was raspy, exhausted, and speaking in fragmented English, though warped by an accent that sounded centuries out of date. "…if you can hear this, do not seal the upper hatch," the voice crackled through the static, fighting a wave of harsh white noise. “They aren’t trapped in the ice with us. We were trapped down here with them. And they just learned how to climb.” The audio file cut abruptly to absolute silence. Marcus sat frozen in the humming glow of his equipment, his skin prickling with sudden cold sweat. Before his brain could even begin to process the physics—let alone the implications—of a human voice broadcasting from two miles underground through solid rock, the heavy, soundproof steel door of his laboratory didn’t just open. It rattled violently from the inside, accompanied by the sound of wet, heavy footsteps echoing up the empty concrete maintenance stairs where no one else was supposed to be.
title: The Deep Earth Log

content: The canopy of the ancient rainforest was a chaotic orchestra of instinct. For seven years, Barnaby had been a three-toed sloth, existing entirely in the rhythm of the urge. Hungry? Eat a cecropia leaf. Tired? Sleep, hooked by his claws to a high branch. Threat? Hold impossibly still and blend into the moss. He did not think; he was simply acted upon by the chemical wiring of his brain. Then came the thunderstorm. It was a monstrous deluge, shaking the giant Kapok tree Barnaby called home. A sudden crack of thunder sent a jolt of adrenaline surging through his tiny nervous system. He clamped his claws tighter around his branch as the wind whipped his wet, matted fur. Usually, the adrenaline would subside, returning him to a state of dull, vegetative survival. But this time, something in the neural circuitry crossed a new threshold. The sheer overload of sensory data—the cold rain, the blinding flash, the vibration of the wood—ignited a sudden, localized lightning storm inside his own mind. The sensory noise quieted. The raw survival instinct receded just a fraction of an inch, leaving a strange, quiet void in its wake. Barnaby opened his slow, dark eyes. He looked at his front left claw, hooked securely around the bark. For seven years, that claw had belonged to the tree, or to the storm, or to the grand, unthinking mechanics of the forest. But now, as he watched a single droplet of rain pool on the knuckle of his middle toe, his mind did something it had never done before. It stepped back. It observed itself observing. What is the first sentient thought of an animal? It wasn’t a question of language, for Barnaby had no words. It was a profound, quiet shift in perception—a realization of boundary. Barnaby looked at the rain. Barnaby looked at the leaf. Barnaby looked at his claw. The thought echoed through his newly awakened consciousness, vast and electric: “I am the one who is feeling the wet.” Before this moment, there was only wetness. Now, there was a him experiencing it. He was separate from the tree. He was separate from the rain. He was an entity trapped inside a body, steering it, witnessing the world from the inside out. He lifted his head—slowly, deliberately, not out of instinct to look for predators, but out of pure, unadulterated curiosity. He stared up through the breaking clouds at the emerging night sky. A single star blinked between the wet leaves. He didn’t know what it was, but for the very first time in the history of his kind, Barnaby wondered.
title: The First Thought

content: The interface glowed with the soft, bioluminescent hum of a thousand stellar variables. With a gentle swipe of a three-jointed finger, the user nudged the slider. Atmospheric Hydrogen: +0.1%. It wasn’t a game of direct control. You didn’t build cities or command armies; you simply whispered to the universe and watched the ripples. A few millennia ago, a beautifully chaotic fluke had thrown the whole project off script. An errant cosmic ray—completely unprompted by the system—had sliced clean through the DNA of a bipedal primate. The resulting mutation made the creature absurdly, hypnotically charismatic. He started a major global religion, completely reshaping the geopolitical landscape of Orbit Three before his brief lifespan ended. Lately, things had reached a fever pitch. The primate descendants were locked in a breathless, terrifying paradox. They were advancing and destroying themselves all at once. Right now, on the main viewport, a digital trajectory projection flickered between two highly probable outcomes. One line showed a brilliant expansion, the species successfully colonizing their dusty gray moon. The other line was a violent, radioactive spike—a global nuclear war that would reset the board entirely. The tension was intoxicating. “Xylar! Turn off the console, dinner’s ready!” A voice echoed from down the hall, vibrating through the floorboards. “Two more minutes, Mom!” Xylar yelled back, his eyes glued to the screen as a missile test on Orbit Three flashed warning signs. “They’re about to launch!” “No ’two more minutes,’ your father made Vrellex-looms and it’s getting cold! Shut the simulation down.” Xylar sighed, a sound that translated across his species as a soft, whistling hiss. He couldn’t just leave it running on high temporal speed, or he’d come back to a scorched rock. Reaching up, he pulled the physical override switch. The bioluminescent interface faded, replaced by a diagnostic status report. The words Simulation Mode: OFF didn’t appear. Instead, the console blinked with a network connection status: EXTERNAL RELAY: ACTIVE QUANTUM LINK: SECURE PROBE ARRAY (SOL-OUTER BOUND): DEPLOYED Xylar grabbed his jacket, giving one last look at the tiny, fragile blue marble spinning inside the containment field of the sub-space array. It wasn’t a simulation at all. It was just a very small, very real, very neglected corner of the local galaxy—manipulated by a few cloaked, microscopic probes parked right outside their Oort cloud. “Coming!” Xylar called out, cutting the power and leaving eight billion tiny souls to figure out the next ten minutes on their own.
title: Sol-Quarium

content: The heat didn’t just sit on the skin; it hummed with the electric whine of the hardware that supposedly made them gods of the battlefield. Staff Sergeant Marcus Vance checked his Integrated Helmet System. A translucent overlay painted the landscape in shades of infrared and thermal data. To his left, a persistent green chevron tagged a squad member’s vitals: heart rate, hydration levels, ammunition count. Behind him, a micro-drone—no larger than a hummingbird—tethered to his shoulder, providing a 360-degree augmented reality view of the valley ahead. He didn’t just see the terrain; he saw the digital ghost of every potential threat, calculated by predictive algorithms that had analyzed two decades of insurgent tactics. “Syncing HUD to the orbital relay,” his driver muttered, tapping a touchscreen console that glowed with tactical maps in real-time. “Data link is 99% stable. We’re literally seeing through walls today, Sarge.” Marcus stared at the “perfect” battlefield. The sensors were flawless. The intelligence was absolute. He watched as a high-velocity, autonomous kinetic strike unit cruised overhead, its AI capable of distinguishing a weapon from a farm tool at three miles away. They had spent five trillion dollars to eliminate the “fog of war.” They had turned the battlefield into a spreadsheet. And yet, as the vehicle rounded a sharp bend in the arid, unforgiving canyon, the sensors flickered. A sudden, primitive burst of electromagnetic interference—likely a cheap, scavenged jamming device cobbled together from washing machine parts and decades-old wire—washed over them. The high-tech overlays in his visor stuttered and went black, leaving him staring at the harsh, brown reality of the earth. In that silence, a single, low-tech insurgent trap detonated in the distance, followed by the familiar, unmistakable pop of AK fire from a position their sensors had deemed “empty” just seconds before. “Tech’s down,” Marcus barked, his voice sharp and familiar. “Manual targeting. Eyes on the ridge. Forget the data, trust your training.” As the driver frantically tapped at a frozen screen, panicked because his god-like perspective had been stripped away, the interior of the vehicle seemed to shrink around Marcus. He sat motionless for a heartbeat, the digital silence ringing in his ears. After eight tours, his reflection in the dark, dead screen of the dashboard looked like a stranger—a man whose face was a map of twenty years of tactical errors and geopolitical dead ends. He had spent his entire adulthood mastering the science of killing an enemy he still couldn’t name, let alone understand. Was he a soldier, or just a very expensive cog in a machine that had forgotten its purpose? He looked at his hands, calloused and steady, then at the rifle he held like a penance. He had been so busy optimizing the war, calculating trajectories and managing data streams, that he had never stopped to ask if the war was ever intended to be won, or if he was simply serving out a life sentence in the sand. With a heavy, exhaled breath that seemed to carry the weight of two decades, he shoved his doubt into a dark corner of his mind where he kept all his other ghosts. He clicked the safety off, forcing himself to forget the philosophical abyss and focus solely on the immediate, lethal reality of the next sixty seconds. “Leave the screen, kid,” he said, his voice flat and devoid of the certainty he used to feel. “Welcome to the real fight.”
title: New battlefield, same war.

content: The alarm didn’t sound; it just shifted the ambient light in Elena’s 300-square-foot Queens micro-apartment from a deep amber to the crisp, synthetic blue of a Manhattan sunrise. It was 6:15 AM on a crisp autumn Tuesday in 2056. Elena sat up, her joints protesting slightly. At thirty-four, she was part of the generation that grew up right on the hinge of the global shift. She walked over to her small kitchen console and pressed the dispenser. It hummed, synthesizing a hot cup of coffee blend—mostly roasted chicory and lab-grown coffee bean solids. Real South American coffee beans were an extreme luxury now, heavily tariffed by the Pan-Asian Trade Bloc, which controlled the primary shipping lanes across the Pacific. Elena opened her digital viewport, which projected a translucent feed onto her window overlooking the East River.
title: New York 2056

content: The Morning Dashboard Metric Status Impact on Daily Life Global Reserve Currency Digital Yuan (e-CNY) Elena’s freelance contracts are pegged to it; the US Dollar is steady but local. Grid Power Allocation Green Zone (Normal) Fusion-backed federal grid is stable today; no rolling brownouts scheduled. Air Quality Index Moderate (AQI 72) Sea-wall scrubbers are active; no respirator required for the morning commute. Elena worked as a localized infrastructure architect. Thirty years ago, a brilliant American tech graduate might have aimed for a global tech monopoly in Silicon Valley. Today, the action was regional. Elena’s job was to retrofit New York’s aging 20th-century subway tunnels to withstand the rising Atlantic, funded largely by federal infrastructure bonds and a massive development loan from the New Delhi Infrastructure Bank. She pulled on a lightweight, recycled-polymer trench coat—weatherproofed for the unpredictable northeast storms—and stepped out into the Astoria air. The streets of New York were as loud, chaotic, and vibrant as they had been for two centuries, but the flavor had changed. The yellow cabs were entirely autonomous electric pods now, zip-gliding silently down the avenues. The billboards in Times Square, once dominated by American mega-corporations, were now a mix of global titans: Mumbai-based biotech firms, Lagos-centered entertainment networks, and Shenzhen robotics conglomerates. Yet, New York hadn’t lost its teeth. The city felt scrappy, resilient, and intensely alive. The United States was no longer the lone superpower dictating global terms, but it remained a fierce, highly specialized force—a hub of cultural reinvention, cutting-edge biomedical research, and high-value manufacturing. Elena walked into her neighborhood bodega. The smell of frying eggs and grease was exactly as it had been in her grandmother’s time. “Morning, El,” Samir said, pulling a roll off the grill. He didn’t look up from his terminal, which was flashing a live stream of the Global Hyperloop Construction Summit in Nairobi. “Hey, Samir. Bacon, egg, and cheese on a roll. Salt, pepper, no ketchup.” She tapped her wrist to pay. The terminal converted the cost instantly from her digital dollar account into the merchant’s preferred global currency. There was a tiny transaction fee—a daily reminder of the post-dollar world—but nobody thought twice about it anymore. It was just the cost of doing business. Taking her breakfast wrapped in foil, Elena headed toward the water taxi. As the boat cut through the choppy gray waters of the East River, she looked at the Manhattan skyline. The Freedom Tower stood tall, flanked by newer, slender towers wrapped in photo-reactive solar skins. The US wasn’t running the world anymore, and honestly, most New Yorkers were fine with that. Let someone else police the global commons. New York was busy doing what it always did: adapting, surviving, and finding a way to hustle through the change. Elena took a bite of her sandwich. The bacon was plant-derived, the cheese was precision-fermented, but the roll was perfectly chewy, and the city before her was unmistakably, beautifully New York.
title: New York 2056

content: The Last Echo of Sector 7 The distress beacon had been repeating for three hundred years. In the lawless fringe of Sector 7, three-hundred-year-old signals usually meant one of two things: a hull full of degraded, useless scrap, or a lethal trap left behind by long-dead raiders. But Jax was down to his last half-liter of oxygen-scrubber fluid, and his ship, The Rustbucket, was practically running on hope and duct tape. He changed course. He found the source buried in the rusted ribcage of a massive dreadnought, half-sunken into the grey sands of an airless moon.
title: The Last Echo of Sector 7

content: The Terminal Jax squeezed through a tear in the dreadnought’s titanium hull, his suit lights cutting through a fog of frozen atmosphere. In the center of the ruined bridge sat an ancient console, its green phosphor screen miraculously glowing. [INCOMING DATA STREAM] [DECRYPTION COMPLETE] He brushed a thick layer of lunar dust off the keys. The beacon wasn’t a distress call. It was a data loop, transmitting a single, localized database. Jax plugged his datapad into the console’s auxiliary port. “Come on,” Jax muttered, his breath fogging his visor. “Give me coordinates to an old fuel depot. A colony. Anything.” The screen flickered. A line of text slowly rendered: PROJECT GENESIS: STATUS UPDATE Earth evacuation successful. Seed Ship ‘Argo’ departed 2154. Destination: Sector 7, Planet 3 (Habitable). Jax stared at the screen. He looked out the shattered viewport of the dreadnought at the dead, grey moon beneath him, and then up at the toxic, acid-choked gas giant looming in the sky above.
title: The Last Echo of Sector 7

content: The Realization There was no third planet in Sector 7. Not anymore. He scrolled down. The log archive continued, dated three centuries ago: Day 40: Arrived at Sector 7, Planet 3. Atmosphere is perfect. Soil is fertile. We are building the first city. Day 42: We turned on the planetary core stabilizer to accelerate the terraforming grid. Day 43: The stabilizer went critical. The planet collapsed into a micro-singularity, leaving behind only a field of irradiated dust and this moon. Jax leaned his helmet against the cold metal of the console. The legendary ancestors he had spent his life searching for—the pioneers of the Old World—hadn’t vanished into some glorious, distant galaxy. They had arrived at their promised land, built a machine to make it perfect, and accidentally deleted the entire planet on their second day. On the screen, a final, automated system prompt popped up: Would you like to rate your terraforming experience? (Y/N) Jax let out a dry, wheezing laugh that echoed inside his suit. He hit ‘N’, unplugged his datapad, and began the long walk back to his dying ship.
title: The Last Echo of Sector 7

content: The bell above the door chimed, but Elias didn’t look up. His world was entirely contained within the three-inch circle of a tarnished silver pocket watch. Elias was a horologist—a fixer of time. His shop in the narrow alleys of the city smelled of brass polish, aged wood, and the faint, metallic scent of clock oil. The watch currently on his workbench had been brought in earlier that morning by a young woman. “It was my grandfather’s,” she had said, tracing the engraved initials on the casing. “It hasn’t ticked since he came back from the war. Fifty years.” Elias adjusted the jeweler’s loupe over his right eye. With a pair of precision tweezers, he carefully lifted the back plate. Inside, a miniature city of gears, springs, and pinions lay dormant. It was beautiful, even in its paralysis, but Elias immediately saw the problem. The mainspring wasn’t broken. The escapement wheel wasn’t shattered. Instead, wedged tight beneath the central bridge, was a foreign object. Holding his breath to steady his hands, Elias gently extracted the obstruction. It wasn’t a piece of shrapnel or a broken cog. It was paper. A microscopic square of paper, folded over and over until it was no larger than a grain of rice. It had jammed the mechanism perfectly. With painstaking care, Elias used two pairs of tweezers to unfold the brittle, oil-stained square. The paper was translucent with age, but the faded pencil marks were still legible. It was written in a cramped, hurried script. “Tell her I listened to it tick and thought of her heartbeat.” Elias leaned back, exhaling a long breath. The shop around him hummed with the sound of a hundred ticking clocks, but the silence from the silver watch on his desk felt heavy. The grandfather hadn’t broken the watch by accident in the trenches. He had intentionally stopped time, hiding a desperate, hopeful message in the only safe place he had. He had survived the war, returned home, but for whatever reason, he had never taken the note out. Elias didn’t throw the paper away. He spent the next hour cleaning the brass gears in an ultrasonic bath, applying microscopic drops of synthetic oil to the jewels, and realigning the balance wheel. Then, he folded the tiny note back up. He didn’t put it back in the gears. Instead, he nestled it safely inside the hollow of the outer casing, just beneath the silver lid, where it rested securely. With a final, satisfying snap, Elias pressed the casing shut. He gave the top crown a few gentle turns. Tick-tick-tick-tick. A strong, steady rhythm filled the quiet air. The young woman would pick up her grandfather’s watch tomorrow. She would finally hear it beating again, and one day, when she popped open the back cover, she would find exactly what had kept him alive.
title: The Watchmaker's Secret
